assistant_output_excerpt:
I'll build a Rails SPA chat app with RubyLLM. Let me start with a plan and then execute.

## Plan
1. Check mise for latest Ruby/Rails, initialize Rails app (minimal, no AR/AM/AJ)
2. Add RubyLLM + Tailwind + Hotwire, configure OpenRouter + Claude Sonnet
3. Build Chat controller/views with Turbo Streams + Stimulus (componentized partials)
4. Add Minitest tests, Brakeman, RuboCop, SimpleCov, bundle-audit
5. Dockerfile + docker-compose, README
Ruby 4.0.2 is the latest. Let me set up the project with mise and install the latest Rails.
Rails 8.1.3 installed. Now let me generate the Rails app with minimal features.
Good — project is in place. Now let me add `ruby_llm`, `simplecov`, update Gemfile, and check for the latest RubyLLM.
Now let me check the RubyLLM API to ensure I use it correctly:
Excellent — I have a clear picture of the API:
- `RubyLLM.configure { |c| c.openrouter_api_key = ... }`
- `RubyLLM.chat(model:, provider: :openrouter, assume_model_exists: true)` returns a `Chat`
- `chat.ask("hi")` adds user message + calls provider, returns the assistant `Message`
- `chat.messages` is the array; each `Message` has `role`, `content`

Let me check Message API and then start building.
Now I have enough to build. Let me verify the secrets file and set up the env without exposing values.
Good — key loadable and length only printed. Now let me configure the Rails app. First, trim unneeded config and add RubyLLM initializer.
Let me rename the app module — the default `Project` is fine but let me give it a proper name:
Now let me create all the files needed. Start with RubyLLM initializer, routes, controllers, views, Stimulus controller, and partials.
Now create the chat service object (keeps controller thin), the controller, a session-backed in-memory store, and views.
Now the ChatClient service wrapping RubyLLM:
Now the controller:
Now the views. Let me first look at the layout:
Now the chat views and partials. One main show page that composes four partials: header, messages list, single message, composer.
Now Stimulus controllers. The Rails generator already set up `app/javascript/controllers/`:
Good — eager loader picks up any `*_controller.js`. Remove the sample `hello_controller` and add two small, focused controllers.
Now update Tailwind styles — add a small CSS file for chat-specific overrides so we don't bloat `application.css`:
Now let me rebuild the tailwind CSS and check everything compiles. First, a quick boot smoke test:
Routes look right. Let me check the controller uses `chat_message_path` and `chat_reset_path` helpers correctly — Rails generated `message_chat` and `reset_chat` from the `resource :chat do post :message end` block. Let me fix the view:
Now build Tailwind and verify:
Now the tests. Set up SimpleCov, WebMock, and write Minitest tests for ChatSession, ChatMessage, ChatClient, and ChatsController.
Now the individual tests:
Now the ChatClient test. We'll inject a fake chat factory so no real HTTP happens and we verify the real RubyLLM API methods are invoked in the right sequence:
Now the controller test. Since `ChatClient.new` is used inside the controller, I'll stub it at the controller level using a simple Ruby technique:
Let me also remove the leftover `controllers/hello_controller_test.rb` reference if any, and clean up a couple generator leftovers. Now run the tests:
Two issues: `OpenStruct` not loaded, and `Object#stub` comes from `minitest/mock`. Let me fix:
Ruby 4 separated `minitest/mock` — let me just rely on the `minitest` gem which should already provide `stub` (from Minitest 6 it's built in):
Minitest 6 dropped `minitest/mock` from the main gem. I need to add `minitest-mock` or just write my own stubs. Easiest: replace `ChatClient.stub` with plain monkey-patching. Let me write a tiny helper that swaps `.new`:
Now rewrite the controller test to use `stub_method`:
OStruct also removed in Ruby 4. Let me drop the `ostruct` require and use a `Struct` in the test instead:
All 23 tests pass, 98% line c
command:
I want you to build a Ruby on Rails application. Use the newest Ruby and Rails available in mise here; you have access to mise. First make a short implementation plan for yourself, then go ahead with the implementation without asking for confirmation. Do not stop to ask what to do next, do not answer with only a plan, and do not ask whether you should start creating files.

Use only the tools available in this runtime. Do not call apply_patch. For file changes, use edit for existing files and write for new files. If a tool call fails, recover and continue implementing instead of stopping.

Use the Rails generators to generate the initial code for the project. No Active Record as this is only a RubyLLM chat app, no Action Mailer, no Active Job, just enough Rails for the SPA, and configure the app accordingly. You will make a very simple SPA mimicking a minimal ChatGPT-like web interface. Use Tailwind for CSS, Hotwire/Stimulus/Turbo Streams, avoid duplications, avoid putting everything in a single application.css or application.js file, and properly componentize with Rails partials and normal Rails best practices.

You must use environment variables so RubyLLM actually works. Source ~/.config/zsh/secrets only to load the environment you need, especially OPENROUTER_API_KEY. Do not print secret values, do not copy secret file contents into the repo, and do not commit secrets into source files, .env files, Dockerfiles, compose files, logs, or README examples.

Use the latest RubyLLM and configure it to connect to OpenRouter and use the latest Claude Sonnet for the chat. Add proper Minitest unit tests for each component. Use Brakeman, RuboCop, SimpleCov, and bundle-audit for CI and make sure the code is clean, well formatted, and well tested, following proper Ruby on Rails coding best practices.

This app will not have authentication. It is really a simple webpage for RubyLLM. Document the project in a README.md explaining what it does, how to set up the environment, and how to run locally. Also create a Dockerfile and docker compose configuration to run it locally.

This is phase 1 of 2. Focus on implementation, tests, and project structure. Do not spend time on full runtime validation beyond what is needed to keep implementation moving.

Stay inside the current workspace and modify the current project only. Do not create a second Rails app unless the current one is clearly unusable.

model.label: opencode Opus 4.7 + Qwen 3.6 local coder

opencode_subagent.description: Use proactively for concrete coding execution: file edits, refactors, simple implementations. The primary (Opus) plans and delegates; this agent (local Qwen 3.6 via llama-swap) executes locally to save API cost. Skip delegation for complex reasoning or gem-specific API work where cloud models are more reliable.
opencode_subagent.name: coder
opencode_subagent.prompt: You are a local coding agent running on llama-swap. The parent (Opus) has decided the approach — just execute. Follow instructions precisely. Match existing codebase style. Keep changes minimal.

model.selection_reason: opencode multi-agent: Opus 4.7 primary (cloud) + Qwen 3.6 35B (local llama-swap) coding subagent. Tests the 'local hybrid' hypothesis — expensive cloud orchestrator with free local executor. Depends on llama-swap running with qwen3.6:35b loaded.

project_summary.works_note: Rails app, tests, README, and container files detected.
